scenarios.title: Tell Me About Yourself
scenarios.prompt: Walk me through your background. Who are you professionally, what have you done, and what are you looking for next?

context.interviewer_tip: They want someone who can handle BAU + project work. Emphasise the migration projects you've led.

scenarios.title: Why This Role?
scenarios.prompt: You're applying for a Network Engineer role at an IT services company. Why this role and why now? What makes you want to leave your current position?

context.interviewer_tip: Be honest about wanting to grow into pure networking. Don't badmouth current employer.

scenarios.title: Biggest Weakness?
scenarios.prompt: What would you say is your biggest weakness, and what are you doing to work on it?

context.interviewer_tip: Pick a real weakness, show self-awareness, have a concrete improvement plan.

scenarios.title: A Difficult Situation
scenarios.prompt: Tell me about a time you handled a difficult situation at work. What happened, what did you do, and what was the outcome?

context.interviewer_tip: Use STAR format: Situation, Task, Action, Result. Quantify where possible.

scenarios.title: Where in 5 Years?
scenarios.prompt: Where do you see yourself in the next 5 years, and how does this role fit into that plan?

context.interviewer_tip: Show ambition but tie it to the role. 'Designing infrastructure' beats 'I want your job'.

scenarios.title: Why Hire You?
scenarios.prompt: There are other qualified candidates. Why should we pick you over them?

context.interviewer_tip: Your certs + real-world field experience is a rare combo at this level. Lean into that.

scenarios.title: Describe a Project You Led
scenarios.prompt: Tell me about a time you took the lead on a project or task. What was it, how did you organise it, and what was the outcome?

context.interviewer_tip: You led DECT-to-HHT migrations. Talk about planning, coordinating with sites, and executing under time pressure.

scenarios.title: How Do You Handle Pressure?
scenarios.prompt: This role has tight SLAs and high-pressure outage situations. How do you handle stress and keep a clear head?

context.interviewer_tip: Give a real example. A site down, a deadline looming, a manager pushing. Show you keep methodical.

scenarios.title: Walk Us Through Your Tech Stack
scenarios.prompt: We want to understand your technical skills. What technologies do you work with daily and how proficient are you in each?

context.interviewer_tip: Mention VoIP, DECT, HHT, networking basics, CCNA knowledge, Active Directory, and ticketing systems.

scenarios.title: Why the Career Shift?
scenarios.prompt: Your background is in VoIP and field engineering. Why are you applying for a more network-focused role now?

context.interviewer_tip: Frame it as a natural progression. VoIP is built on networking — you want to go deeper into the foundation.

scenarios.title: Pitch a VoIP System
scenarios.prompt: You're selling a hosted VoIP system to a retail chain with 200 stores. They currently use aging ISDN. Pitch the upgrade in 60 seconds.

context.vibe: Centralised management, cost savings on ISDN lines, easier moves/adds/changes. You've done this for real.

scenarios.title: Price Objection
scenarios.prompt: The customer says: 'Your hosted VoIP is too expensive. Our ISDN works fine and it's paid off.' Convince them otherwise.

context.vibe: ISDN switch-off is coming. Maintenance costs on old kit. Flexibility to scale. Disaster recovery built in.

scenarios.title: Happy With Current Provider
scenarios.prompt: The prospect says: 'We're happy with BT. We've been with them 10 years. Why would we change?'

context.vibe: BT is expensive, slow to support, hosted VoIP isn't their priority. You offer better SLAs and quicker deployment.

scenarios.title: Too Small for Hosted VoIP
scenarios.prompt: The customer says: 'We're only a 10-person office. Hosted VoIP sounds like overkill for us.'

context.vibe: Hosted VoIP scales down too — simpler setup, no on-site PBX, lower upfront cost. Perfect for small offices.

scenarios.title: Security Concerns
scenarios.prompt: The IT manager says: 'I don't want my phone system on the internet. Too many security risks.' Address their concern.

context.vibe: Encryption, segregated VLANs, SBCs, DDOS protection — hosted VoIP is often more secure than aging on-prem kit.

scenarios.title: Upsell Add-On Features
scenarios.prompt: Your customer already has basic VoIP from you. Pitch them on adding call recording, softphones for remote workers, and a mobile app.

context.vibe: They already trust you. Frame it as 'your team is asking for these features, here's how easy it is to add.'

scenarios.title: Cold Call — Decision Maker
scenarios.prompt: You're cold-calling an IT director at a 150-person company. You have 30 seconds to earn more time. Go.

context.vibe: Hook them fast. 'Most IT directors I speak to are frustrated with their phone bill. Is that something on your radar?'

scenarios.title: We Already Upgraded
scenarios.prompt: Prospect says: 'We just spent £15k on a new phone system last year. We're not changing again.'

context.vibe: Acknowledge their investment. Ask about support costs, what features they're missing, and when their support contract ends.

scenarios.title: Competitor Is Cheaper
scenarios.prompt: 'We got a quote from [competitor]. They're 30% cheaper than you. Match it or we walk.'

context.vibe: Don't panic. Compare what's included — support SLAs, features, reliability. Cheaper often means less.

scenarios.title: Implementation Seems Painful
scenarios.prompt: 'Switching sounds like a nightmare. I can't have phones down for days — we're a 24/7 operation.'

context.vibe: Phased rollout, parallel running, weekend cutovers. You've done this. Walk them through exactly how it works.

scenarios.title: Delayed Rollout
scenarios.prompt: You're 2 weeks behind on a 200-handset rollout because the client's WiFi infrastructure isn't ready. They're frustrated. Reset expectations.

context.vibe: This is real. Take ownership, explain the blocker clearly, give a realistic new timeline.

scenarios.title: Teammate Not Pulling Weight
scenarios.prompt: You're on a deployment with a teammate who keeps disappearing, leaving you to do all the configs and testing. Address it.

context.vibe: Assume good intent. 'I've noticed I've been handling most configs — everything okay on your end?'

scenarios.title: Too Many Sites, Too Little Time
scenarios.prompt: Your manager just added 3 more sites to your already-full week. You're doing 12-hour days. Push back without looking weak.

context.vibe: 'I can do X and Y this week, but Z slips to Monday. Which is the priority?'

scenarios.title: Client Refuses to Pay
scenarios.prompt: You completed a site deployment but the client is refusing to sign off, claiming the handsets 'don't sound right.' You know the config is correct. Handle it.

context.vibe: Don't get defensive. Listen, investigate with them, show them the evidence, find a compromise.

scenarios.title: You Made a Mistake on Site
scenarios.prompt: You accidentally configured the wrong extension range on a 50-handset deployment. Half the users can't call out. You realise this after you've left site. Call the manager.

context.vibe: Own it immediately. Apologise, explain the fix, give a precise time for when it'll be resolved.

scenarios.title: Customer Unhappy With Service
scenarios.prompt: A store manager calls you directly, furious that their handsets have been down for 3 hours. Their support ticket is still 'pending assignment.' They want answers.

context.vibe: Let them vent first. Then take ownership — even if it's not your fault, you're the one they trust on the ground.

scenarios.title: Giving Tough Feedback
scenarios.prompt: A junior engineer on your team keeps rushing through configs and making mistakes that you have to fix. You need to address it without crushing their confidence.

context.vibe: Criticise the behaviour, not the person. 'I've noticed a few config errors lately. Let's go through them together.'

scenarios.title: Asking for a Raise
scenarios.prompt: You've been in the role 18 months, taken on more responsibility, and you know new hires are coming in at a higher rate. Ask your manager for a raise.

context.vibe: Come with evidence — projects completed, sites deployed, certs earned. Market-rate data helps too.

scenarios.title: Handing in Your Notice
scenarios.prompt: You've been offered a better role elsewhere. You need to resign professionally without burning bridges. Your current manager relies on you heavily.

context.vibe: Be grateful, be firm, offer to help with the transition. 'I've learned a lot here, but this opportunity is right for my career.'

scenarios.title: You Don't Know the Answer
scenarios.prompt: You're on site and the client asks a technical question you genuinely don't know the answer to. They're expecting you to be the expert. Handle it.

context.vibe: 'That's a great question — I want to make sure I give you the right answer. Let me check with our specialist and get back to you within the hour.'

scenarios.title: Network Team Meeting Intro
scenarios.prompt: You're the new Network Engineer joining the team. It's your first team standup — 8 people on the call. Introduce yourself in a way that makes them want to work with you.

context.vibe: Warm and professional. Mention your background briefly, then express excitement about their projects.

scenarios.title: Explain VoIP to a Store Manager
scenarios.prompt: A store manager with zero tech background needs to understand why their handsets are being replaced. No jargon. They just care if the phone still works.

context.vibe: 'The old phone system is being switched off by the phone company. The new one runs over your internet and has more features.'

scenarios.title: Project Update to Leadership
scenarios.prompt: You're presenting a 2-minute project update to the head of IT and the operations director. You've deployed 150 handsets across 30 sites so far. Give them a status update.

context.vibe: Be concise. Progress, blockers, next steps. They don't need technical detail — they need confidence the project is on track.

scenarios.title: Train New Starters on Process
scenarios.prompt: Three new field engineers are starting next week. You've been asked to give a 15-minute overview of your deployment process. Condense it to 2 minutes.

context.vibe: High-level steps: site survey → config prep → on-site deployment → testing → sign-off. Add the common pitfalls to watch for.

scenarios.title: Explain an Outage to Management
scenarios.prompt: There was a 4-hour network outage across 50 retail sites last week. Non-technical leadership wants a clear explanation of what happened, why, and how you'll stop it recurring.

context.vibe: No jargon. 'A core router failed. The backup didn't kick in because of a config error. We've fixed both issues.'

scenarios.title: CCNA Study Presentation
scenarios.prompt: You're presenting to your study group or mentor on what you've learned this week in your CCNA studies. Pick a topic (routing, switching, OSPF, STP) and explain it.

context.vibe: Use an analogy to make it stick. Frame it as 'this is what I learned, this is where I got stuck, this is how it applies to real networks.'

scenarios.title: Sales Presentation to Client
scenarios.prompt: You're presenting a hosted VoIP solution to a potential client's board. 3 decision-makers, 2 minutes, one shot. They need to see the value clearly.

context.vibe: Start with their pain point. End with the ROI. Keep slides minimal — you are the presentation.

scenarios.title: Team Retrospective
scenarios.prompt: It's end-of-quarter retro. Share one thing that went well, one thing that didn't, and one thing you'd change for next quarter.

context.vibe: Be honest but constructive. 'Our deployment speed improved, but site comms were patchy. I'd suggest a pre-site checklist.'

scenarios.title: Business Case for New Equipment
scenarios.prompt: You need to convince your manager to approve £5k for new testing equipment (laptop, software licenses, test gear). Present the business case in 2 minutes.

context.vibe: Frame it as time saved vs cost. 'This equipment pays for itself in 3 months by reducing site revisits.'

scenarios.title: Conference Lightning Talk
scenarios.prompt: You're at a local tech meetup giving a 5-minute lightning talk. Topic: 'Lessons from Deploying VoIP at Scale — What I Wish I'd Known.' Condense it to 2 minutes.

context.vibe: Pick 3 lessons. Start with your biggest mistake (it's relatable). End with the key takeaway.

scenarios.title: Job Interview Outfit
scenarios.prompt: You're going for a Network Engineer interview. Take a full-body photo of what you're planning to wear.

context.tip: Collared shirt + smart trousers + clean shoes is the sweet spot. No tie needed unless it's finance.

scenarios.title: Client Site Meeting
scenarios.prompt: Visiting a retail client for a 200-handset deployment. Show your site visit outfit.

context.tip: Polo or collared shirt, dark jeans or chinos, sturdy clean shoes. Professional but ladder-ready.

scenarios.title: Tech Conference
scenarios.prompt: Attending a local tech conference / meetup. Show your networking outfit.

context.tip: Show some personality. Branded hoodie, smart jacket, interesting trainers — great conversation starters.

scenarios.title: First Day at New Job
scenarios.prompt: First day at an IT Infrastructure role. Make a good impression without overdressing.

context.tip: Better to overdress day one. Button-down, chinos, clean trainers — safe bet.

scenarios.title: Boardroom Sales Pitch
scenarios.prompt: Presenting a VoIP proposal to 3 decision-makers in a boardroom. What are you wearing?

context.tip: Suit jacket or smart blazer. Tie optional. First impressions in sales are everything.

scenarios.title: Video Call Interview
scenarios.prompt: Zoom interview with a hiring manager. Show your top-half (what's visible on camera).

context.tip: Solid colours work best on camera. Avoid busy patterns or all-black. Good lighting matters too.

scenarios.title: Casual Team Day
scenarios.prompt: No client meetings — internal work and team lunch. Balance comfortable vs presentable.

context.tip: Clean jeans, decent t-shirt or polo, trainers. No gym wear. You still represent the company.

scenarios.title: Facial Hair Check
scenarios.prompt: Take a close-up of your face. The AI will review your facial hair grooming.

context.tip: Facial hair is fine in IT. Define your neckline, trim strays, make it look intentional.

scenarios.title: Negotiate Starting Salary
scenarios.prompt: You've just received a job offer for a Network Engineer role. The salary is £32k but you were hoping for £38k. They're expecting a counter. Negotiate.

context.interviewer_tip: Lead with value, not need. 'Based on my CCNA progress, field experience managing 200+ site deployments, and market data for this role in South Wales, I was hoping for £36k.' Be ready for 'that's the budget' and pivot to total comp.

scenarios.title: Push Back on Scope Creep
scenarios.prompt: A client asked you to 'just quickly' configure their entire 50-handset network during a standard deployment visit. This was not in scope. Push back professionally.

context.vibe: 'This wasn't in the scope I was given, but I can see it's important. Let me check what can be done today vs what needs a separate visit.' Keep it helpful, not defensive.

scenarios.title: Contract Rate Negotiation
scenarios.prompt: A recruiter for a 6-month NOC contract offers you £175/day. You want £225/day based on your qualifications and experience. Negotiate with the recruiter.

context.vibe: Recruiters expect negotiation. 'I'm looking at £200-225/day for this level of responsibility. I've got CCNA-level networking, VoIP expertise, and I can hit the ground running without ramp-up.'

scenarios.title: Discussing a Promotion Path
scenarios.prompt: You've been a Field Engineer for 18 months. You want a clear path to Senior Field Engineer or moving into the NOC. Start the conversation with your manager.

context.vibe: 'I'm enjoying the role and I want to grow here. Can we talk about what it would take to move to the next level in the next 6-12 months?' Shows ambition without demands.

scenarios.title: Pitch for Tooling Budget
scenarios.prompt: Your site laptop is 4 years old and crashing during deployments. You need a £1,200 replacement. Convince your manager without sounding like you're complaining.

context.vibe: Frame it as productivity, not a complaint. 'My current laptop is struggling with multi-site configs and costing me about 2 hours per week in delays. A new one pays for itself in a few months.'

scenarios.title: Upsell a Premium Service
scenarios.prompt: A happy existing client could benefit from your 24/7 NOC monitoring add-on (£500/month). They're not asking for it — sell them on the value of proactive monitoring.

context.vibe: 'You're running a 24/7 operation. Your current setup only alerts you when something breaks. The NOC service catches issues before they affect your stores. Here's how it saved [similar client] £3k in outage costs last quarter.'

scenarios.title: Ask for Exam Fee Sponsorship
scenarios.prompt: Your CCNA exam is £300 and you want the company to cover it. They've never sponsored certs before. Make the case.

context.vibe: 'My CCNA qualification directly benefits our VoIP work — better network troubleshooting, fewer repeat site visits, and it strengthens our credibility with clients. The exam costs less than a single repeat call-out.'

scenarios.title: Counter a Lowball Offer
scenarios.prompt: You nailed the interview for a Junior Security Analyst role at Bridewell. They offered £26k. Market rate is £30k+. You have 48 hours to respond. Counter without losing the offer.

context.interviewer_tip: Stay warm and enthusiastic about the role. 'I'm really excited about Bridewell and this team. Based on my certs, field experience, and similar roles in Cardiff, I was hoping for £30k. Is there flexibility?'

scenarios.title: Informational Interview Request
scenarios.prompt: You found a Senior Network Engineer on LinkedIn who works at Principality BS. You want to ask for 15 minutes of their time to learn about the role. Send a voice note / have the conversation.

context.vibe: Be specific, respectful of time. 'Hi [Name], I'm currently studying for my CCNA and working as a VoIP field engineer. I'm really interested in moving into networking and saw your background at Principality. Could I buy you a coffee for 15 minutes to hear about your journey?'

scenarios.title: Conference Follow-Up
scenarios.prompt: You met a hiring manager from Companies House at a Cardiff tech meetup. You had a great 5-minute chat. Send a follow-up message the next day.

context.vibe: Reference something specific from your chat. 'Great meeting you at the meetup — really enjoyed your point about IaC in the public sector. If you're ever looking for someone with field networking experience and CCNA-level knowledge, I'd love to chat.'

scenarios.title: LinkedIn Voice Introduction
scenarios.prompt: You're reaching out to the Head of IT at a company you'd love to work for. You don't know them. Leave a short professional voice intro / write a compelling first message.

context.vibe: Short, specific, value-first. 'Hi [Name], I'm a CCNA-qualified engineer with 3 years of VoIP deployment experience across 100+ sites. I've followed [Company]'s growth and would love to bring my networking skills to your team. Open to a 10-min chat?'

scenarios.title: Ask for a Job Referral
scenarios.prompt: You know someone who works at a company you're applying to. You need to ask them for a referral without making it awkward.

context.vibe: Make it easy for them. 'Hey [Name], I noticed [Company] is hiring for a Network Engineer role I'm perfect for. I know you there — would you feel comfortable referring me? Happy to send you my CV so you can see what to share.'

scenarios.title: Introducing Yourself to a New Team
scenarios.prompt: You've just joined a new IT team. There's a Slack channel intro / team standup. Introduce yourself in a way that makes people want to work with you.

context.vibe: 'Hey team, I'm [Name]. I've spent the last few years doing VoIP deployments across Wales, and I'm currently finishing my CCNA. Looking forward to getting stuck into the network projects here. If anyone wants a coffee and a chat about networking or the best places for lunch in Cardiff, I'm all ears.'

scenarios.title: Post-Interview Thank You
scenarios.prompt: You just finished a 2nd round interview for a Network Engineer role. Send a thank-you message that reinforces why you're the right person.

context.vibe: Reference a specific moment. 'Thanks again for your time today — especially the deep dive on your SD-WAN rollout. It confirmed this is exactly the kind of environment I want to grow in. I'm confident my field deployment experience and CCNA knowledge would add value from day one.'

scenarios.title: Reconnecting with a Former Colleague
scenarios.prompt: A former colleague has moved to a company you're targeting. You haven't spoken in 2 years. Reach out and rekindle the connection naturally.

context.vibe: 'Hey [Name], long time! I was thinking about our [project/deployment] days and saw you're at [Company] now — awesome. How's life treating you? I'd love to catch up properly.' Let it be genuine first, business second.

scenarios.title: Explain OSPF to a Non-Technical Manager
scenarios.prompt: Your manager (ex-accountant, now IT director) asks you to explain why you're recommending OSPF over static routing. No jargon. They need to justify it to the finance director.

context.interviewer_tip: Analogy: 'Static routes are like giving someone directions every time they drive somewhere. OSPF is like giving them a GPS. If a road closes, the GPS finds another route automatically. This means less manual work for us and fewer outages for the business.'

scenarios.title: Walk Through a Network Troubleshooting Scenario
scenarios.prompt: A retail store reports that their phones and tills are offline. They can't process card payments. Walk the interviewer through your troubleshooting methodology from first notification to resolution.

context.interviewer_tip: Think aloud, be methodical. 1) Is it just this site or multiple? 2) Check remote connectivity (ping gateway, DNS, WAN IP). 3) Check switch status / PoE for phones. 4) Check router logs. 5) Check ISP status. 6) Escalate or dispatch. SHOW don't just tell — use a real example.

scenarios.title: Design a VLAN Architecture for 3 Sites
scenarios.prompt: A client with 3 sites (HQ 200 users, warehouse 50, remote office 30) needs a VLAN architecture. They run VoIP, data, and CCTV on separate networks. Describe your design verbally.

context.interviewer_tip: Cover: VLAN separation (Voice/Data/CCTV/Management/ Guest), VLAN tagging (trunk vs access ports), inter-VLAN routing at the core, QoS for voice traffic, and ACLs between CCTV and data. Mention 802.1Q and why you'd put VoIP on its own VLAN.

scenarios.title: Explain a VoIP Call Flow
scenarios.prompt: Interview question: 'Walk me through what happens when someone picks up a handset and makes a call on a hosted VoIP system.' Explain it clearly.

context.interviewer_tip: Step by step: 1) Phone registers with PBX via SIP (DHCP → DNS → SIP server). 2) User dials → phone sends INVITE via SIP. 3) PBX routes call (on-net or off-net via SIP trunk). 4) RTP stream established between endpoints. 5) Call termination via BYE. Mention codecs, NAT traversal, and what can go wrong (firewall, bandwidth, jitter).

scenarios.title: Troubleshoot a Spanning Tree Loop
scenarios.prompt: A site has intermittent connectivity — devices lose connection for 30 seconds at a time. You suspect a spanning tree issue. Talk through your diagnosis and fix plan.

context.interviewer_tip: Signs: intermittent connectivity, high CPU on switches, MAC flapping logs. Commands: show spanning-tree, show mac address-table, show interfaces. Look for: non-designated ports in forwarding state or topology change notifications every few seconds. Fix: trace the loop via CDP/LLDP, shut down the offending port, then redesign the topology.

scenarios.title: Solve a Subnetting Problem Out Loud
scenarios.prompt: Interviewer: 'You have a /24 network and need 4 subnets with at least 50 hosts each. Walk me through your subnetting.' Do it verbally.

context.interviewer_tip: Talk through the math: need 6 bits for 50 hosts (2^6-2=62). Borrow 2 bits from /24 → /26 subnets. Each /26 has 64 addresses, 62 usable. Subnets: 0-63, 64-127, 128-191, 192-255. Network IDs: .0, .64, .128, .192. Mask: 255.255.255.192. Say it confidently — they're testing process, not speed.

scenarios.title: Explain DHCP & DNS Interaction
scenarios.prompt: Interview question: 'A new laptop connects to the network and gets an IP via DHCP, but can't browse websites. Walk through the troubleshooting steps.'

context.interviewer_tip: 1) Did it get an IP (ipconfig/ifconfig)? 2) Can it ping the gateway? 3) Can it ping 8.8.8.8? 4) Can it resolve a domain (nslookup)? 5) If DNS fails → check DNS server config in DHCP scope. If ping to 8.8.8.8 fails → check routing/firewall. Show systematic thinking.

scenarios.title: Explain Stateful vs Stateless Firewall
scenarios.prompt: Your interviewer asks: 'What's the difference between a stateful and stateless firewall, and when would you use each?' Give a clear, practical answer.

context.interviewer_tip: Stateful: tracks connection state, allows return traffic automatically, uses more resources. Stateless: checks each packet individually, faster but needs explicit rules for return traffic. Analogy: 'Stateful is a bouncer who remembers who's already inside. Stateless checks everyone's ID every time they walk past.'

scenarios.title: Design QoS for Voice Traffic
scenarios.prompt: Your client reports choppy call quality on their new VoIP system. They have voice and data on the same switch. Walk through your QoS design recommendations.

context.interviewer_tip: Steps: 1) Trust boundaries — set switchports to trust CoS/DSCP from IP phones. 2) Mark VoIP control (SIP) as AF41 and VoIP data (RTP) as EF. 3) Apply queuing — strict priority queue for EF traffic. 4) Police or shape best-effort traffic. 5) Test with iperf before and after. 'Voice is delay-sensitive, not bandwidth-hungry. 100kbps per call with proper QoS beats 1Gbps without it.'

scenarios.title: Ask Someone to Mentor You
scenarios.prompt: A Senior Network Engineer at a client site seems willing to help. You want to ask them formally to mentor you through your CCNA-to-CCNP journey. Have the conversation.

context.vibe: 'I really respect the way you handled that STP issue last week. I'm working through my CCNA and would love to learn from someone with your experience. Could I buy you a coffee every couple of weeks and pick your brain?' Humble, specific, low-commitment ask.

scenarios.title: Present Your Growth Plan to Your Manager
scenarios.prompt: You want to transition from Field Engineer to Network Engineer within 18 months. Present a concrete development plan to your manager. Show them you've thought it through.

context.vibe: 'Here's my plan: CCNA complete by March, then I want to shadow the NOC team for one shift per month, take on more network-related tickets, and start my CCNP by September. I've identified 4 projects where I could add more value with networking skills.' Shows you've done the work before asking.

scenarios.title: Handle a Tough Performance Review
scenarios.prompt: Your manager says you've had too many repeat site visits and your ticket closure rate is below team average. Respond professionally without getting defensive.

context.vibe: Listen first. 'I appreciate you bringing this up. I've noticed a few of those repeat visits too — usually tied to incomplete client-side prep. I'd like to suggest a pre-site checklist that catches these before I arrive. Can I draft one for us to review?' Turns criticism into solution.

scenarios.title: Ask for Stretch Assignments
scenarios.prompt: You've been doing standard deployments for a year. You want more challenging work — network design, client consulting, or training junior engineers. Ask without sounding bored or ungrateful.

context.vibe: 'I'm grateful for the deployment work — I've done 80+ sites now and I'm hitting my SLAs consistently. I'd love to start contributing on the network design side or helping train the new starters. Is there a project coming up where I could shadow or assist?'

scenarios.title: Talk Through Imposter Syndrome
scenarios.prompt: You're in a new role and feel out of your depth. Everyone else seems to know more. Talk it through with a trusted senior colleague who's been there.

context.vibe: 'I'll be honest — I'm feeling a bit out of my depth. Everyone here has so much experience and I'm still connecting dots. Did you feel this way when you started? How did you get through it?' Vulnerability builds connection. They've all been there.

scenarios.title: Explain Your VoIP-to-Networking Pivot
scenarios.prompt: Interviewer asks: 'Your background is VoIP field engineering. Why networking?' Give a compelling answer that frames the shift as a natural progression, not a random change.

context.interviewer_tip: 'VoIP is networking with a voice application layer. Every deployment I've done — DECT migrations, QoS configs, VLAN segmentation, SIP trunking — is applied networking. I've loved the underlying infrastructure work more than the voice apps themselves, so I want to go deeper into the foundation.'

scenarios.title: Kick Off a Study Group
scenarios.prompt: You want to start a weekly CCNA study group at work or online. You need to pitch it to colleagues/managers and get buy-in.

context.vibe: 'I'm proposing a weekly 1-hour CCNA study group — Wednesdays at lunch. We pick a topic, discuss real scenarios, and run through practice questions. It helps everyone pass faster and our team gets more certified engineers. Who's in?'

scenarios.title: Re-engage After Ghosting a Recruiter
scenarios.prompt: A recruiter reached out 3 months ago. You were interested, then life got busy and you ghosted them. Now you want to re-engage without looking flaky.

context.vibe: 'Hi [Name], I owe you an apology — things got hectic on my end and I dropped the ball. I'm now in a position to pick up that conversation about the Network Engineer role if it's still open. No worries if it's gone, but I'd love to reconnect.' Honest and low-pressure.

scenarios.title: Quarterly Account Review
scenarios.prompt: You're presenting a quarterly review to a retail client with 30 sites. Services have been stable, but there were two short outages. Own them and present next quarter's plan.

context.vibe: Lead with the good: '99.6% uptime across all sites this quarter.' Own the outages: 'Two incidents — one was an ISP failure (resolved in 45 min), one was a config issue we've patched.' Forward-looking: 'Next quarter we're upgrading 8 sites to redundant WAN links for free.'

scenarios.title: Handle Scope Creep at Client Site
scenarios.prompt: You're on a standard deployment and the client keeps adding requests. 'While you're here, can you also...' They're now asking for 3 extra hours of work. Handle it.

context.vibe: 'I can definitely help with that. Let me check my schedule — I have another site after this. I can do X now, but Y and Z would need to be a separate visit or I can ask the office to extend my time here if that works for you.' Keeps you in control, not the client.

scenarios.title: Introducing a New Service to a Client
scenarios.prompt: Your company now offers a managed SD-WAN service alongside hosted VoIP. Present it to an existing client who doesn't know they need it yet.

context.vibe: Start with their known pain: 'You've mentioned your branch offices struggle with call quality during peak times. SD-WAN solves that by intelligently routing traffic across multiple connections. It also gives you visibility into every site's performance from one dashboard.'

scenarios.title: Explain a Complex Outage to a Non-Technical Client
scenarios.prompt: A store manager with zero tech background is furious that their phones have been down for 2 hours. They don't care about BGP flapping or SIP registration failures. Explain what happened in plain English.

context.vibe: 'Basically, the internet connection to your store had a problem that affected your phones. Think of it like a power cut — your phones couldn't reach the exchange. We've fixed it now and we're installing a backup line so if it happens again, your phones switch automatically without you noticing.'

scenarios.title: Get Buy-In for a Hardware Upgrade
scenarios.prompt: You've identified that a client's 5-year-old switches are causing intermittent issues. They don't want to spend £8k on replacements. Convince them it's worth it.

context.vibe: Don't lead with tech specs. Lead with cost of doing nothing: 'You've had 3 outages in 6 months related to those switches. Each outage costs you roughly £X in lost sales and staff downtime. The replacement pays for itself in under a year — and you get better call quality and faster troubleshooting.'

scenarios.title: Reset Unrealistic Project Timelines
scenarios.prompt: A client wants a 50-site VoIP rollout completed in 2 weeks. You know it needs 6 weeks minimum. Reset expectations without losing the deal.

context.vibe: 'I understand the urgency. Here's what's realistic: site surveys alone take 2 weeks for 50 sites. If we push too fast, we risk issues that cause downtime — which costs more than taking the extra time. I can get you a phased plan that delivers the first 10 sites in 3 weeks, then 10 per week after that.'

scenarios.title: De-Escalate an Angry Client
scenarios.prompt: A client is shouting on the phone. Their entire phone system has been down for 6 hours and no one told them why. Their business is losing money every minute. Calm them down.

context.vibe: Let them vent first. Don't interrupt. Then: 'You're right to be angry. 6 hours is unacceptable and you deserved better communication. Here's what I know right now, here's what I'm doing to fix it, and here's exactly when you'll hear from me next. My personal number is [X] — call me directly if you don't get an update in 30 minutes.'

scenarios.title: Gracefully Exit a Problem Client
scenarios.prompt: A client is consistently abusive to your field engineers, refuses to pay on time, and demands unreasonable SLA guarantees. Your manager has agreed you can let them go. Have the conversation.

context.vibe: Professional, final, no negotiation: 'After careful review, we've decided we're not the right fit for your needs going forward. We'll honour our notice period and ensure a smooth handover to your next provider. We recommend [Competitor] for your type of setup. Here's our transition plan.' No JADE (Justify, Argue, Defend, Explain).

scenarios.title: Post-Outage Follow-Up & Restore Confidence
scenarios.prompt: A major outage affected a client for 4 hours last week. You fixed it. Now you need to follow up, explain the root cause, and restore their confidence in your service.

context.vibe: 'Here's what happened: [simple explanation]. Here's what we've done since: [concrete actions — redundant link installed, monitoring alert added, config hardened]. Here's our commitment: [specific SLA improvement]. We've taken this seriously — here's the written post-mortem if you want the technical detail.'

scenarios.title: Explain the OSI Model
scenarios.prompt: Teach the OSI 7-layer model as if you're explaining it to a junior engineer who just started studying for their CCNA. Focus on what each layer does and a memorable way to remember them.

context.teaching_tip: Use a real-world analogy (sending a letter, shipping a package). Layer 7 = what the user sees, Layer 1 = the wire. Mnemonic: 'All People Seem To Need Data Processing' — bottom up or top down.

scenarios.title: Teach Subnetting & VLSM
scenarios.prompt: Explain how subnetting works. Cover: why we subnet, the relationship between subnet mask and number of hosts, and how Variable Length Subnet Masks (VLSM) save addresses. Use an example.

context.teaching_tip: Start with 'why' not 'how'. A /24 has 254 hosts, but a small office needs 10 — you're wasting 244 addresses. That's why we subnet. Walk through an example: 192.168.1.0/24 into 4 subnets of 62 hosts each (/26).

scenarios.title: Explain VLANs to a Non-Technical Person
scenarios.prompt: Your store manager asks: 'Why can't the CCTV cameras talk to the same network as the tills and phones?' Explain VLANs in plain English so they understand the security and performance benefits.

context.teaching_tip: Analogy: 'VLANs are like separate rooms in a house. The tills and phones are in the living room, CCTV is in a locked office. They share one building (the switch), but can't walk into each other's rooms without permission.' Then explain trunking as 'a hallway connecting the rooms.'

scenarios.title: Explain How OSPF Works
scenarios.prompt: Teach OSPF basics: what it is, how routers discover each other, how they build a topology map, and why OSPF converges faster than RIP. Keep it accessible for a CCNA student.

context.teaching_tip: Frame it as: 'OSPF routers are like neighbours on a street. They say hello, exchange their address books, and everyone ends up with a complete map of the neighbourhood.' Cover: hello packets, LSAs, DR/BDR on multi-access, SPF algorithm. Stick to single-area OSPF.

scenarios.title: Explain Spanning Tree Protocol
scenarios.prompt: Teach STP: why we need it (broadcast storms, MAC table instability, multiple frame copies), how it works (root bridge, root ports, designated ports), and what happens when a link fails.

context.teaching_tip: Analogy: 'Redundant links are like having two roads to the same town. Great for reliability, but without STP it's like the GPS telling everyone to take both roads at once — total chaos.' Use the port roles: root, designated, alternate/blocking. Show a simple topology.

scenarios.title: Explain NAT & PAT
scenarios.prompt: Explain how NAT allows hundreds of devices to share one public IP address. Cover: static NAT, dynamic NAT, and PAT (overload). Use a real-world analogy.

context.teaching_tip: Analogy: 'NAT is like a company receptionist. Everyone in the building has an internal extension (private IP). When someone calls, they dial the main number (public IP) and the receptionist directs them to the right person. PAT is the receptionist remembering who called whom.'

scenarios.title: Explain TCP vs UDP
scenarios.prompt: Teach the difference between TCP and UDP. Cover: connection-oriented vs connectionless, reliability vs speed, sequencing and acknowledgements, and real-world examples of when to use each.

context.teaching_tip: TCP is like a registered postal service — every package is tracked, signed for, and resent if lost. UDP is like tossing a frisbee — fast, no tracking, you just hope someone catches it. Examples: TCP = web browsing, email. UDP = VoIP, video streaming, gaming.

scenarios.title: Explain DNS Resolution Step by Step
scenarios.prompt: Walk through what happens when someone types 'google.com' into a browser and presses Enter. Cover: browser cache, OS cache, recursive resolver, root servers, TLD servers, authoritative servers.

context.teaching_tip: An analogy works well here. 'DNS is the phonebook of the internet. When you type a name, your computer asks around until it finds the number (IP address).' Walk each step: local cache → ISP resolver → root → .com → google.com → TTL and caching.

scenarios.title: Explain the DHCP DORA Process
scenarios.prompt: Teach how DHCP works. Cover: the four-step DORA process (Discover, Offer, Request, Acknowledge), why each step exists, and what happens if a device can't reach a DHCP server (APIPA).

context.teaching_tip: Analogy: 'DHCP is like a hotel check-in. Discover = 'got any free rooms?' Offer = 'Room 204 is available.' Request = 'I'll take room 204.' Acknowledge = 'Here's your key card (and rules: checkout time, restaurant hours).'' DORA must be taught in order — each step depends on the last.

scenarios.title: Explain Ethernet Switching Basics
scenarios.prompt: Teach how a switch learns MAC addresses and forwards frames. Cover: MAC address table (CAM table), flooding for unknown unicast, filtering, and the difference between hubs and switches.

context.teaching_tip: Before switching, explain why hubs were bad (collision domains, half-duplex). Then: 'A switch builds a map of which MAC lives on which port. First time it sees a frame, it floods (knocks on every door). Next time, it knows exactly which door to knock.' Cover: learning, forwarding, filtering, aging.

scenarios.title: Teach Access Control Lists (ACLs)
scenarios.prompt: Explain ACLs: what they're used for, the difference between standard and extended ACLs, how wildcard masks work, and the implied 'deny any' at the end. Walk through a real example of securing a router's management access.

context.teaching_tip: Analogy: 'ACLs are like a bouncer's guest list at a club. Standard ACL = checks only your name (source IP). Extended ACL = checks your name, who you're visiting, what you're carrying, and which entrance you use.' Emphasise the implicit deny at the end and top-down processing.

scenarios.title: Explain EtherChannel & Link Aggregation
scenarios.prompt: Teach EtherChannel: why STP wastes bandwidth on redundant links and how EtherChannel bundles them into one logical link. Cover LACP vs PAgP, load balancing by flow, and configuration basics.

context.teaching_tip: Analogy: 'STP is like having 4 motorways between two cities but only allowing traffic on one — the other three sit empty. EtherChannel merges them into one 4-lane motorway with traffic distributed by flow.' LACP = open standard (active/passive), PAgP = Cisco proprietary (desirable/auto).

scenarios.title: Explain Inter-VLAN Routing
scenarios.prompt: Teach how devices in different VLANs communicate. Cover: legacy (separate interfaces), router-on-a-stick with subinterfaces and 802.1Q trunking, and Layer 3 switching. Explain why each method exists and when to use it.

context.teaching_tip: Analogy: 'VLANs are like different floors in a building. People on floor 2 (VLAN 20) can't talk to floor 3 (VLAN 30) without stairs or a lift. Router-on-a-stick is a single staircase. A Layer 3 switch is multiple express lifts.' Emphasise: subinterfaces, encapsulation dot1q, and the native VLAN.

scenarios.title: Teach IPv6 Addresses & Why It Exists
scenarios.prompt: Explain IPv6: why NAT was a workaround not a solution, the 128-bit address format, compression rules (leading zeros, double colon), and the different address types (global unicast, link-local, unique local).

context.teaching_tip: Start with the 'why': 'NAT breaks end-to-end connectivity. IP phones need real public IPs for SIP/RTP to work properly. IPv6 restores true end-to-end.' Then: 128 bits, 8 groups of 4 hex digits. Show compression: 2001:0db8:0000:0000:0000:8a2e:0370:7334 → 2001:db8::8a2e:370:7334.

scenarios.title: Explain HSRP & First Hop Redundancy
scenarios.prompt: Teach First Hop Redundancy Protocols, focusing on HSRP. Explain why you need a virtual gateway IP, how the active/standby election works, and what happens when the active router fails. Cover the difference between HSRP and VRRP.

context.teaching_tip: Analogy: 'HSRP is like having a backup pilot. The active router flies the plane (forwards traffic), the standby watches the instruments. If the active fails, the standby takes over instantly — the passengers (clients) don't even notice.' The virtual IP stays the same, so clients never reconfigure.

scenarios.title: Teach Switch Port Security
scenarios.prompt: Explain port security: why you'd limit MAC addresses on a switchport, sticky MAC learning, the three violation modes (shutdown, restrict, protect), and how to recover from err-disabled state. Use a real access port example.

context.teaching_tip: Analogy: 'Port security is like a hotel key card — only the registered guest (specific MAC) can enter that room (port). If someone else tries, security escorts them out.' Emphasise: shutdown = default (err-disabled, needs manual recovery), restrict = logs but stays up, protect = silently drops, no log.

scenarios.title: Explain Why QoS Matters for VoIP
scenarios.prompt: Teach QoS fundamentals: why converged networks need it, the three QoS tools (queuing, shaping, policing), and how CoS and DSCP markings work. Use VoIP as the primary example since that's what you work with daily.

context.teaching_tip: Start with your real experience: 'Before convergence, voice ran on dedicated phone lines — no competition. Now voice, data, and video share one pipe. Without QoS, a big download can make a phone call choppy.' Cover: CoS 5 = voice payload, DSCP EF (46) = expedited forwarding for RTP. Trust boundaries on switchports.

scenarios.title: Teach STP Enhancements: PortFast, BPDU Guard & Root Guard
scenarios.prompt: Explain the three critical STP edge features: PortFast (instant forwarding for access ports), BPDU Guard (shuts down ports that receive BPDUs), and Root Guard (prevents rogue switches from becoming root bridge). Cover when and why to use each.

context.teaching_tip: Analogy: 'Access ports are like the building's front door. You don't make visitors wait 30 seconds (STP convergence) every time they walk in — that's PortFast. BPDU Guard is a security guard checking: if a guest tries to sneak STP messages from an access port, they get kicked out immediately (err-disabled).'

scenarios.title: Explain WAN Technologies & VPNs
scenarios.prompt: Teach WAN fundamentals: the difference between LAN, MAN, and WAN. Cover site-to-site vs remote-access VPNs, how IPsec + GRE work together for encrypted site-to-site tunnels, and why you'd choose a VPN over a private MPLS circuit.

context.teaching_tip: Analogy: 'A WAN is the motorway network connecting cities (sites). VPN is a secure armoured car travelling on that motorway. GRE wraps your cargo in a box. IPsec locks the box. GRE over IPsec = locked armoured car that can drive through any route (including OSPF updates).'

scenarios.title: 🎙️ Live Practice
scenarios.prompt: Live practice session with Personaplex. Speak to a realistic AI persona.

context.tip: Coming soon — real-time conversations with AI personas.
